---
title: "Agentic engineering"
author: "F. Szedleski & C. Combe"

format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 10pt
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    auto-stretch: false
    scrollable: true
    width: 1400
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    transition: fade
    smaller: false
    mermaid:
      theme: dark
      config:
        fontFamily: "Inter, Segoe UI, Arial, sans-serif"
---

# One Mental Model

<style>
/* Let wide dataframe-style tables scroll horizontally instead of squeezing columns. */
.reveal .table-scroll {
  width: 100%;
  overflow-x: auto;
}
.reveal .table-scroll table {
  min-width: max-content;
  margin: 0;
}
.reveal .table-scroll th,
.reveal .table-scroll td {
  white-space: nowrap;
}

.reveal .slash-command-table .table-scroll {
  width: auto;
  max-width: 100%;
}

.reveal .slash-command-table .table-scroll table {
  width: auto;
  min-width: 0;
  table-layout: auto;
}

.reveal .slash-command-table .table-scroll col {
  width: auto !important;
}

.reveal .slash-command-table .table-scroll th,
.reveal .slash-command-table .table-scroll td {
  width: auto;
  white-space: normal;
}


.reveal .slide img.section-zero-image {
  max-width: none !important;
  max-height: none !important;

}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .callout {
  margin: 0 0 1rem 0;
  border-radius: 22px;
  border: 2px solid transparent;
  box-shadow: 0 16px 36px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.24);
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .callout .callout-title-container {
  letter-spacing: 0.04em;
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .callout .callout-body-container {
  padding-bottom: 0.7rem;
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .callout p {
  margin: 0.35rem 0;
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .callout code {
  background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.09);
  border-radius: 999px;
  padding: 0.14rem 0.55rem;
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .chat-view-card {
  border-color: #23c7b5;
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(35, 199, 181, 0.22), rgba(13, 18, 27, 0.96));
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .inline-chat-card {
  border-color: #f59e0b;
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(245, 158, 11, 0.22), rgba(20, 17, 12, 0.96));
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .quick-chat-card {
  border-color: #f97316;
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(249, 115, 22, 0.22), rgba(24, 15, 12, 0.96));
}

.reveal .chat-surface-grid .agent-mode-card {
  border-color: #38bdf8;
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(56, 189, 248, 0.22), rgba(12, 17, 25, 0.96));
}
</style>

<script>
document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => {
  for (const table of document.querySelectorAll(".reveal .slide table")) {
    if (table.parentElement?.classList.contains("table-scroll")) {
      continue;
    }

    const wrapper = document.createElement("div");
    wrapper.className = "table-scroll";
    table.parentNode.insertBefore(wrapper, table);
    wrapper.appendChild(table);
  }
});
</script>

This presentation explains:


- Your **code**, your rules with custom-instructions & memories
- **Agents** as specialists that can reason and act
- **Skills** and **Hooks** as the specialisation layer
- **MCP** as the context extension layer
- **Sub-agents** & **Workflows** as the coordination model
- **Plugins** as the distribution layer for skills, hooks & mcp

# Part 0 : setting the scene {#part-0}

## How a modern LLM gets built

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/whats_a_llm.png){fig-align="center" .section-zero-image}

An LLM product is a stack, not a single training run.

- **Dataset + preprocessing** decide what signal enters the model
- **Pre-training** builds the general world model
- **Post-training** makes it follow instructions and preferences
- **Optimization + distillation** make it cheap, fast, and deployable

The behavior you see in chat is shaped by the whole pipeline.

## The core architecture is not the surprise anymore

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/llm_arch_no_change.png){fig-align="center" .section-zero-image}

The last two years did not require a brand-new architecture every quarter.

- transformer-style designs still dominate
- gains came from better data, post-training, inference, and tooling
- the stack around the model improved faster than the core model shape

That matters because teams can build operating practices around a moving frontier without waiting for a totally new paradigm.

## Capability kept moving anyway

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/always_more_complex_tasks.png){fig-align="center" .section-zero-image}

Even with a familiar architecture, frontier capability moved fast enough to change daily engineering work.

- task horizons keep extending from seconds to minutes and hours
- models can stay on-task long enough to matter on real software workflows
- the question is no longer "can a model help?" but "which slice of work can it finish reliably?"

## The frontier keeps being repriced

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/model_race.png){fig-align="center" .section-zero-image}

- benchmark leaders keep rotating
- model choice is now an operational decision, not a once-a-year platform decision
- teams need an operating model that survives rapid model churn

## The frontier is jagged

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/jagged_frontier.png){fig-align="center".section-zero-image}

Raw model power is not the same as reliable execution.

- similar-looking tasks can fall on opposite sides of the frontier
- decomposition, context, and framing can move work from outside to inside the frontier
- this is why workflow design matters as much as model choice

## Impact is already visible, but uneven

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/llm_impacts.png){fig-align="center" .section-zero-image}

Anthropic's usage data points to task-level augmentation, not a flat automation story.

- highest exposure is concentrated in knowledge work
- observed usage is lower than theoretical coverage
- software, business, legal, education, and office work are already in scope

The shift starts where work is language-heavy, iterative, and tool-mediated.

## The builder pool is expanding beyond engineers

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/claude_code.png){fig-align="center" .section-zero-image}

This is the practical shift:

- useful internal tools can now be built by domain experts
- the person closest to the problem can automate part of it
- AI-assisted coding is becoming a general problem-solving interface

The point is not that everyone became a software engineer. The point is that software creation is becoming more accessible.

## Why learn AI-assisted coding now

If the architecture is stable, the capability curve is rising, the frontier is jagged, and more people can build, then the differentiator is no longer access to a model.

The differentiator is knowing how to:

- give the model the right context
- constrain how it acts
- connect it to real tools and systems
- verify that its output is actually correct

That is what the rest of this deck is about.

# Part 1 : your code, your rules {#part-1}
## Team Instructions Matter

`.github/copilot-instructions.md` gives every run the same baseline.

- coding standards
- testing expectations
- security rules
- error-handling conventions

Without shared instructions, each run improvises. With them, output becomes consistent.

---

## Set up your workspace for AI with `/init`

With always-on custom instructions like `copilot-instructions.md` or `AGENTS.md`, you can ensure that the AI uses your project's conventions for generating code.

To prime your project with an initial set of instructions based on your codebase, you can now use the **/init** slash command in chat.


- **Discovers** existing AI conventions in your workspace
- **Analyzes** your codebase structure and patterns
- **Generates** comprehensive, tailored workspace instructions
- **Customizable:** `/init` is implemented as a contributed prompt file, allowing you to modify its underlying behavior

---

## The Memory Tool

The **memory tool** allows agents to save and recall notes as they work. You can explicitly ask the agent to remember something or retrieve it. All data is stored locally on your machine.

*Enabled via:* `github.copilot.chat.tools.memory.enabled`

| Scope | Path | Persists Session | Persists Workspace | Use for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User** | `/memories/` | Yes | Yes | Preferences, patterns, commands |
| **Repository** | `/memories/repo/` | Yes | No | Codebase conventions, structure |
| **Session** | `/memories/session/` | No | No | Task context, in-progress plans |

---

## Using Memory Scopes


- **User memory**: Auto-loaded (first 200 lines) across all projects.
  - *Prompt: "Remember that I prefer tabs over spaces"*
- **Repository memory**: Scoped to the current workspace and persists across sessions.
  - *Prompt: "Remember that all API endpoints require authentication"*
- **Session memory**: Scoped to the current conversation.
  - *Example: The Plan agent uses this to persist its implementation plans (`plan.md`).*

---

## Managing Memories

Store/retrieve memories simply by talking to the agent (*"What are our commit message conventions?"*).

**Available VS Code Commands:**
- `Chat: Show Memory Files`
- `Chat: Clear All Memory Files`

*(Note: Deleting individual memory files is not yet supported. Clear all, or ask the agent to update a specific file to remove outdated information.)*

# Part 2: What is an Agent?

## The Basic Loop

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4
%%| fig-height: 3
%%{init: {'flowchart': {'htmlLabels': false}} }%%
flowchart LR
    U["User request"] --> A["Agent"]
    A --> C["Context"]
    A --> T["Tools"]
    A --> D["Decision"]
    D --> A
```

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}


1. The agent perceives the **request**
2. The **context** determines what the model can see
3. The **tools** determine what the model can do
4. The agent decides **what to do next**

:::
::::

---

## Agent Types in VS Code

VS Code uses the word **agent** for 2 related but different things:

- **Built-in agent modes**: product-defined runtimes with their own tool loop
- **Custom agents**: reusable personas you define in `.agent.md` files

### Built-In Runtime Modes

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="25%"}
#### Plan Agent

- planning-first workflow before coding
- explores codebase context and constraints
- drafts phased steps and validation
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
#### Coding Agent

- edits files
- runs commands and tools
- tests and iterates
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
#### Third-Party Agent

- supplied by external providers like Codex or Claude
- may expose its own tool loop and behaviors
- still runs in the same VS Code chat surface
- built-in GitHub Copilot auth & billing
:::

::: {.column width="25%"}
#### Custom Agent

- user- or team-defined persona in the picker
- can restrict tools and prefer a model
- can hand off to other agents
- defined in a `.agent.md` file

::: {.fragment}
```yaml
---
name: security-reviewer
tools: ["search", "github"]
model: GPT-5 (copilot)
---
```
:::
:::
::::

Key distinction:

- **Plan, Coding, Third-Party** = runtime harnesses
- **Custom Agent** = reusable persona layer

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/agent.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

Built-in and third-party agents are mostly **runtime + tool harness**.

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/agent-loop.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

---

## Custom Agents in VS Code

### What They Actually Are


- Unlike the built-in modes above, a custom agent is a reusable persona defined in a `.agent.md` file
- It bundles instructions, allowed tools, optional subagents, and model preferences
- It is persistent: you switch to it from the agents dropdown instead of re-prompting the same behavior every time
- It can be user-facing, subagent-only, or both


Use a custom agent when you need a stable role such as planner, solution architect, security reviewer, or release manager.

---

### Where Agent Files Live

| Scope | Default location | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Workspace | `.github/agents/*.agent.md` | shared with the repo and team |
| User profile | `~/.copilot/agents/*.agent.md` | reusable across projects |


- `chat.agentFilesLocations` can add more workspace discovery paths
- parent-repository discovery can be enabled for monorepos


---

### Frontmatter That Changes Behavior

| Field | Why you care |
|---|---|
| `description` | discovery text and picker hint |
| `tools` | least-privilege tool access |
| `agents` | which subagents this agent may invoke |
| `model` | fixed or prioritized model selection |
| `user-invocable` | show or hide in the picker |
| `disable-model-invocation` | allow or block use as a subagent |
| `handoffs` | guided transitions to the next role |

The body of the file is plain Markdown instructions. Those instructions are prepended when the agent is active.

---

### Practical Agent Example

```yaml
---
name: planner
description: Generate an implementation plan before coding
tools: ["search", "web", "agent"]
agents: ["implementation", "security-reviewer"]
model: GPT-5 (copilot)
handoffs: [{ label: Start Implementation, agent: implementation, prompt: Implement the approved plan above. }, { label: Run Security Review, agent: security-reviewer, prompt: Review the implementation for security regressions. }]
---

You are an expert system architect and planner.
Before writing any code, your goal is to break the user's
request down into actionable chunks.

1. Analyze the dependencies of the requested feature
2. Create a step-by-step implementation guide
3. Identify potential security risks
4. Do not generate code—instead, hand off to the implementation agent
```

This is more than prompt text. It defines who the agent is allowed to be and what it is allowed to do.

---

### Where Agents Run

Agents are no longer constrained just to your active interactive chat window. They can be executed in the background for long-running or batch tasks.

![Agent Execution Modes](agent-types-diagram-v3.png){fig-align="center" width="60%"}

---

### What Agents can do

AI-powered capabilities can execute actions autonomously. VS Code provides built-in protections:

- **Trust Boundaries**: Explicit consent required for Workspaces, Extension publishers, MCP servers, and Network domains.
- **Scope & Isolation**: Workspace-limited file access, Session-level temp permissions, Background agent Git worktree isolation.
- **Approvals & Review**: Diff editor reviews for file changes, explicit tool and terminal approvals.
- **Sandboxing**: Terminal (`chat.tools.terminal.sandbox.enabled`) and MCP server sandboxing restrict file system and network access.
- **Hooks**: Use `PreToolUse` hooks to deterministically block dangerous operations (e.g., `rm -rf`).
- **Enterprise Policies**: Centralized controls to disable agents, restrict extension tools, or manage MCP server sources.

# Part 3: Skills and Hooks

## Skills Package Reusable Expertise

| Skill type | Location |
|---|---|
| **Project skills** (repository) | `.github/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, `.agents/skills/` |
| **Personal skills** (user profile) | `~/.copilot/skills/`, `~/.claude/skills/`, `~/.agents/skills/` |

**Example structure:**
```text
.github/skills/
├── security-audit/
├── performance-profiling/
└── api-design/
```


- A skill is a reusable capability folder (or a single file)
- It can include instructions, scripts, and supporting resources
- Agents load skills when a task needs that capability
- The interoperable format is documented in the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification)
- `SKILL.md` contains YAML frontmatter plus Markdown instructions
- Required fields: `name`, `description`; optional fields include `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata`, and `allowed-tools`

---

## Agent Skills Directory Structure

A skill is a directory containing, at minimum, a `SKILL.md` file.

```text
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md          # Required: metadata + instructions
├── scripts/          # Optional: executable code
├── references/       # Optional: documentation
├── assets/           # Optional: templates, resources
└── ...               # Any additional files or directories
```

- `SKILL.md` is the entry point an agent reads first
- Supporting code, docs, and templates can live alongside it

---

## Agent Skills Frontmatter

::: {.slash-command-table}
| Field | Required | Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | Yes | Max 64 characters. Lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only. Must not start or end with a hyphen. |
| `description` | Yes | Max 1024 characters. Non-empty. Describes what the skill does and when to use it. |
| `license` | No | License name or reference to a bundled license file. |
| `compatibility` | No | Max 500 characters. Indicates environment requirements such as intended product, system packages, or network access. |
| `metadata` | No | Arbitrary key-value mapping for additional metadata. |
| `allowed-tools` | No | Space-separated string of pre-approved tools the skill may use. Experimental. |
:::

**Minimal example:**
```markdown
---
name: code-review
description: Review code for security, correctness, and maintainability issues.
---
```

Source: [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification)

---

## Community Skills Resources

Great examples of community-driven skill repositories:


- **Airflow skills:** [astronomer/agents](https://github.com/astronomer/agents?tab=readme-ov-file#skills)
- **dbt skills:** [dbt-labs/dbt-agent-skills](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-agent-skills)
- **Python skills:** [honnibal/claude-skills](https://github.com/honnibal/claude-skills)
- **Duckdb skills:** [duckdb/claude-skills](https://github.com/duckdb/duckdb-skills)

---

## Hooks Shape Agent Behavior

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4
%%| fig-height: 5
%%{init: {'flowchart': {'htmlLabels': false}} }%%
flowchart TD
    SS(["SessionStart"]) --> UPS["UserPromptSubmit"]
    UPS -. Context full .-> PC["PreCompact"]
    UPS --> PLAN["Agent Reasoning"]
    PC --> PLAN

    PLAN --> PRE["PreToolUse"]
    PRE --> RUN["Execute Tool"]
    RUN -. success .-> POST["PostToolUse"]
    RUN -. failure .-> ERR["OnError"]
    POST --> PLAN
    ERR --> PLAN

    PLAN --> SSTART["SubagentStart"]
    SSTART --> SSTOP["SubagentStop"]
    SSTOP --> PLAN

    PLAN --> OUT["Return result"]
    OUT --> STOP(["Stop"])
```
:::

::: {.column width="55%"}

| Hook Event | Best Used For |
|---|---|
| **SessionStart** | Init state, validate workspace |
| **UserPromptSubmit** | Audit reqs, inject system context |
| **PreToolUse** | Block danger operations, get approval |
| **PostToolUse** | Auto-format, validate tool output |
| **PreCompact** | Save context before truncation |
| **SubagentStart/Stop** | Track nested agents, aggregation |
| **Stop** | Generate reports, cleanup |
:::
::::

---

## Hook Example

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "npx prettier --write \"$TOOL_INPUT_FILE_PATH\""
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

This turns a manual cleanup step into an automatic rule.

# Part 4: MCP

## MCP Connects Agents to Real Systems

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 6
%%| fig-height: 2.5
%%{init: {'flowchart': {'htmlLabels': false}} }%%
flowchart LR
    VS["VS Code host"] --> GH["GitHub MCP"]
    VS --> CM["Custom MCP"]
    GH --> GHR["PRs<br/>issues<br/>repos"]
    CM --> CMR["internal tools<br/>APIs<br/>docs"]
```


- MCP exposes resources and tools
- Agents can query systems directly instead of relying on pasted context
- This is what turns chat into a connected engineering interface


---

## MCP Flow in Practice

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 6
%%| fig-height: 4
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant A as Agent
    participant S as GitHub MCP
    U->>A: Check if PR 123 has conflicts
    A->>S: getPRDetails(pr=123)
    S-->>A: conflicts=true, files affected
    A-->>U: PR has conflicts and here is where
```

---

## What a Custom MCP Server Can Expose


- employee and org data
- infrastructure actions
- finance or operational systems
- internal documentation and runbooks


The important point is not the server itself. The important point is that the agent can act with live context.

---

## Adding an MCP Server via UI

VS Code provides built-in UI commands to easily manage MCP servers.

**Steps:**

1. Open the Command Palette (`Ctrl+Shift+P` / `Cmd+Shift+P`).
2. Type and select `MCP: Add Server`.
3. Choose **Install** (see below) to add a new server (or browse the ones available).

---

## Adding an MCP Server Manually

You can manually define an MCP server in your workspace config file. The location and format depend on your tool:

**VS Code:** `.vscode/mcp.json` with `"servers"` key
**Copilot CLI:** `.mcp.json` with `"mcpServers"` key

**Example Configuration:**
```json
{
    "servers": {
        "my-custom-server": {
            "command": "python",
            "args": [
                "/path/to/my_server.py"
            ],
            "env": {
                "API_KEY": "${API_KEY}"
            }
        }
    }
}
```

⚠️ **Security Note:** Never hardcode secrets in `.vscode/mcp.json`. Instead, inject credentials via:
- Environment variables (e.g., `export API_KEY=...`)
- Local `.env` file (excluded from version control)
- Secure credential stores (OS Keychain, 1Password, Vault)

After modifying, agents will instantiate the server and have access to its resources and tools!

---

## Setting up Common MCP Servers

Adding an MCP server mostly involves configuring an `mpx` package or a custom standalone script with specific env vars.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### GitLab
```json
{
  "servers": {
      "GitLab": {
        "command": "npx",
          "args": [
                  "mcp-remote",
                  "https://gitlab.com/api/v4/mcp",
                  "--static-oauth-client-metadata",
                  "{\"scope\": \"mcp\"}"
                  ]
                }
              }
}
```
### Atlassian
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "atlassian": {
      "type": "sse",
      "url": "https://mcp.atlassian.com/v1/sse",
      "command": ""
    }
  }
}
```
### Snowflake
```json

  {
  "servers": {
    "mcp-server-snowflake": {
      "command": "uvx",
      "args": [
        "snowflake-labs-mcp",
        "--service-config-file",
        "<path_to_file>/tools_config.yaml",
        "--connection-name",
        "default"
      ]
    }
  }
}

```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Airflow
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "airflow": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "bash",
      "args": [
        "-c",
        "AIRFLOW_API_URL=${AIRFLOW_API_URL} AIRFLOW_AUTH_TOKEN=${AIRFLOW_AUTH_TOKEN} uvx astro-airflow-mcp --transport stdio"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```
*Note: Set `AIRFLOW_API_URL` and `AIRFLOW_AUTH_TOKEN` environment variables before running. Do not commit credentials to version control.*

### AWS
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "awslabs.aws-serverless-mcp-server": {
      "command": "uvx",
      "args": [
        "awslabs.aws-serverless-mcp-server@latest",
        "--allow-write",
        "--allow-sensitive-data-access"
      ],
      "disabled": false,
      "autoApprove": []
    }
}}
```
### Microsoft IQ (Graph) - https://github.com/microsoft/work-iq
*(Coming soon via official MS endpoints, typically authorized through identity providers like Entra ID rather than plaintext configs).*
:::
::::

# Part 5: Sub-Agents, Workflows & Orchestration

## Agents vs Subagents

| Concept | Who starts it | Main purpose | Persistence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | the user selects it | own the conversation and workflow | persistent persona and config |
| **Subagent** | another agent invokes it | solve one focused task and return a result | isolated run inside a parent workflow |


- A subagent is not a different primitive by itself; it is an agent used from inside another agent
- Custom agents can serve as subagents unless `disable-model-invocation: true`
- `user-invocable: false` hides an agent from the picker while still allowing subagent use


---

## Implementing Parallel Work

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 6
%%| fig-height: 4
%%{init: {'flowchart': {'htmlLabels': false}} }%%
flowchart TD
    M["Security Audit"] --> S1["Static Code Analysis"]
    M --> S2["Dependency Vulnerability Check"]
    M --> S3["Secrets Scanning"]
    M --> S4["Compliance Review"]
    S1 --> X["Aggregated Security Report"]
    S2 --> X
    S3 --> X
    S4 --> X
```

- Use sub-agents when tasks can be split cleanly
- Parallel work reduces waiting time
- Isolation helps avoid context bleed and conflicting edits

## Parallel Work Prompt Pattern

You can run subagents programmatically by prompting the agent to invoke them.
Inside your `.agent.md` instructions, ask the model to use the agents concurrently:

```markdown
# Instructions
You are the Lead Security Auditor.

To complete a full review, invoke your subagents concurrently on the provided PR diff:
1. Use the Static Analyzer agent to check for insecure code patterns.
2. Use the Dependency Checker agent to verify package versions against CVEs.
3. Use the Secrets Scanner agent to ensure no keys or tokens are in the diff.

Wait for all agents to return their outputs, then compile their findings into a single unified Markdown report.
```

### Key Benefits

- **Context efficiency:** Subagents operate in dedicated context windows, preventing overflow.
- **Specialization:** Different agents can use models optimized specifically for their task.
- **Parallel execution:** Independent tasks run concurrently across multiple subagents.

---

## Orchestration In Practice: Copilot Orchestra

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 6
%%| fig-height: 3.3
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Conductor
    participant Planning
    participant Implement
    participant Review

    User->>Conductor: Request feature/change
    Conductor->>Planning: Gather context
    Planning-->>Conductor: Return findings
    Conductor->>User: Present plan
    User-->>Conductor: Approve plan

    loop For each phase
        Conductor->>Implement: Execute phase (TDD)
        Implement-->>Conductor: Report completion
        Conductor->>Review: Review implementation
        Review-->>Conductor: Return status

        alt Approved
            Conductor->>User: Present summary & commit message
            User-->>Conductor: Commit & continue
        else Needs Revision
            Conductor->>Implement: Revise with feedback
        else Failed
            Conductor->>User: Request guidance
        end
    end

    Conductor->>User: Plan complete
```
*Copilot Orchestra sequence by @ShepAlderson*

**Community Examples:**

- **Copilot Orchestra:** A multi-agent framework orchestrating a complete development cycle (see preceding slide).
- **GitHub Copilot Atlas:** An extended system with specialized agents like "Prometheus" (planning), "Oracle" (research), "Sisyphus" (implementation), and "Explorer" (rapid discovery).

# Part 6: Plugins

## Plugins Bundle Capabilities

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5
%%| fig-height: 4
%%{init: {'flowchart': {'htmlLabels': false}} }%%
flowchart TD
    P["Plugin"] --> SK["Skills"]
    P --> PR["Prompts"]
    P --> MCP["MCP config"]
    P --> HK["Hooks"]
    P --> CMD["Commands"]
```

Plugins are distribution units. They make a working setup reusable.

---

## Agent vs Skill vs Plugin
::: {.slash-command-table}
| Concept | What it is | Typical use |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | A persistent role with instructions and tool policy | plan, code, review |
| **Skill** | A reusable capability | audit, profiling, validation |
| **Plugin** | A packaged collection | share a full setup across teams |
:::
If you need a reusable persona with tool restrictions or handoffs, use an agent. If you need portable expertise that many agents can load on demand, use a skill.

# Part 7: Model Updates

## Current Copilot Model Snapshot

As of late May 2026, the GitHub changelog and current Copilot model docs point to this lineup.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**OpenAI**

- GPT-5 mini
- GPT-5.3-Codex
- GPT-5.4
- GPT-5.4 mini
- GPT-5.5

**Anthropic**

- Claude Haiku 4.5
- Claude Sonnet 4.5, 4.6
- Claude Opus 4.6, 4.7, 4.8
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Google / GitHub**

- Gemini 2.5 Pro
- Gemini 3 Flash *(preview)*
- Gemini 3.1 Pro *(preview)*
- Gemini 3.5 Flash
- Raptor mini *(preview)*

**Recent changelog highlights**

- **May 28:** Claude Opus 4.8 became GA in Copilot
- **May 19:** Gemini 3.5 Flash became GA in Copilot
- **May 17:** GPT-5.3-Codex became the base model for Copilot Business and Enterprise
- **May 20:** Auto model selection in VS Code started routing by task
:::
::::

*Model availability varies by client, plan, and policy, so treat the picker as a live surface rather than a fixed yearly list.*

---

## Retired And Closing Models

GitHub's current retirement history and May changelog entries changed the picture again.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**Closing on June 1, 2026**

- GPT-4.1
- GPT-5.2
- GPT-5.2-Codex

**Retired in May 2026**

- **May 15:** Grok Code Fast 1 -> GPT-5 mini
- **May 7:** Claude Sonnet 4 -> Claude Sonnet 4.6
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Earlier 2026 retirements**

- **Apr 15:** GPT-5.1 -> GPT-5.3-Codex
- **Apr 1:** GPT-5.1-Codex, GPT-5.1-Codex-Max, GPT-5.1-Codex-Mini -> GPT-5.3-Codex
- **Mar 26:** Gemini 3 Pro -> Gemini 3.1 Pro
- **Feb 17:** GPT-5 -> GPT-5.2
- **Feb 17:** GPT-5-Codex -> GPT-5.2-Codex
- **Feb 17:** Claude Opus 4.1 -> Claude Opus 4.6
:::
::::

*The practical lesson: model names churn faster than team conventions, so keep custom agents and demos aligned with the current picker.*

---

## Reasoning Levels And Billing

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### What You Can Tune

- In VS Code and Copilot CLI, some models support **configurable reasoning levels**
- Supported examples include `Claude Sonnet 4.6`, `Claude Opus 4.6`, `Claude Opus 4.7`, `Claude Opus 4.8`, `GPT-5.3-Codex`, `GPT-5.4`, and `GPT-5.5`
- The same family of models can also support an extended **1 million token** context window
- Use regular reasoning by default, then increase it when a task genuinely needs deeper multi-step analysis
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Why This Matters Now

- GitHub Copilot moves to **usage-based billing on June 1, 2026**
- Model choice and token consumption are converted into **AI credits**
- Higher reasoning levels and larger context windows consume more AI credits
- Code completions and next edit suggestions remain unlimited on paid plans
:::
::::

*Teach the operating habit, not just the model names: pick the model for the task, raise reasoning only when needed, and watch cost once AI credits go live.*

---

![](vscode_agents_concepts_files/libs/slides/llm-costs.png){fig-align="center" width="95%"}

# Key Takeaways

## Which Extension Point Fits?

- Want Copilot to **always** follow team rules: use **custom instructions**
- Want a **repeatable workflow** you invoke when needed: use a **skill**
- Want Copilot to work with **external systems**: add an **MCP server**
- Want **guardrails or automation** around tools and session events: use **hooks**
- Want a **specialist reviewer** or safer permissions: define a **custom agent**
- Want Copilot to split off **complex work**: let it use **subagents**
- Want to ship the whole setup to a team: package it as a **plugin**

Think in terms of scope:

- instructions shape everything
- skills shape one kind of task
- hooks shape lifecycle behavior
- MCP changes capability
- agents shape specialization
- plugins shape distribution

### Useful Repositories To Explore

- [github/spec-kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)
- [microsoft/hve-core](https://github.com/microsoft/hve-core)
- [github/awesome-copilot](https://github.com/github/awesome-copilot)
- [astronomer/agents](https://github.com/astronomer/agents)
- [obra/superpowers](https://github.com/obra/superpowers)

# Part 8: GitHub Copilot Chat in VS Code {#part-8}

## Where Chat Shows Up In VS Code

Copilot Chat is the user-facing surface where instructions, agents, tools, and MCP show up in daily work.

:::: {.columns .chat-surface-grid}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout-note .chat-view-card appearance="simple"}
### Chat View

**Best for:** longer conversations in the secondary side bar  
**Shortcut:** `Ctrl+Alt+I`
:::

::: {.callout-tip .inline-chat-card appearance="simple"}
### Inline Chat

**Best for:** asking from the editor or terminal without leaving the file you are working in  
**Shortcut:** `Ctrl+I`
:::
:::

::: {.column width="100%"}
::: {.callout-important .quick-chat-card appearance="simple"}
### Quick Chat

**Best for:** fast one-off questions without changing your layout  
**Shortcut:** `Ctrl+Shift+Alt+L`
:::
:::
::::

This is the front door for many of the capabilities from Parts 1 to 6.

---

## Tools And Context In Chat

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Add Relevant Context

- **Add Context...** attaches files, symbols, selections, commits, problems, and test failures
- `#file`, `#folder`, and `#symbol` ground the prompt in specific workspace artifacts
- Dragging files, folders, or Problems entries into chat is often faster than re-explaining the issue
- `#` mentions add context or a specific tool directly from the prompt box
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Let Chat Use Tools

- Built-in tool sets cover read, search, edit, execute, web, and subagent delegation
- MCP servers and extensions can add more tools and live system access
- Tools change what Copilot can **do**, not just what it can **say**
- Permission levels still matter: agent mode is most powerful when approvals and scope are configured intentionally
:::
::::

---

## Slash Commands Worth Teaching

::: {.slash-command-table}
| Slash command | Best used for |
|---|---|
| `/[prompt-name]` | run a reusable prompt file in chat |
| `/[skill-name]` | run an agent skill in chat, for example `/webapp-testing` |
| `/chronicle:*` | query session history, generate standups, and get usage tips from local chat history |
| `/clear` | start a fresh chat session |
| `/compact` | summarize a long conversation to preserve context budget |
| `/create-agent` | generate a custom agent with AI assistance in Agent mode |
| `/create-hook` | generate a hook configuration with AI assistance in Agent mode |
| `/create-instruction` | generate an instructions file with AI assistance in Agent mode |
| `/create-prompt` | generate a prompt file with AI assistance in Agent mode |
| `/create-skill` | generate an agent skill with AI assistance in Agent mode |
| `/doc` | generate inline documentation or comments from the editor |
| `/explain` | explain a code block, file, or concept |
| `/fix` | repair a selected problem, compiler error, or failing code path |
| `/init` | create or refresh workspace instructions from the project structure |
| `/plan` | produce an implementation plan before coding |
| `/tests` | generate or extend tests for selected code |
| `/troubleshoot` | analyze agent debug logs for the current chat session or a prior one with `#session` |
:::

The key distinction from the next section: these are VS Code Chat commands, not Copilot CLI commands.

`/troubleshoot` requires `github.copilot.chat.agentDebugLog.enabled`.

---

### [Chronicle (Experimental)](https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_118#_chronicle-experimental)

See [Query session history with chronicle](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/chat/session-insights) for the full reference.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
#### Set It Up

1. Enable `github.copilot.chat.localIndex.enabled`.
2. Keep using Copilot Chat normally. Chronicle records session metadata, conversation turns, files touched, and PR, issue, and commit references in a local SQLite database.
3. Start querying that history once you have a few sessions worth of data.
4. Run `/chronicle:reindex` if sessions are missing or the local index needs to be rebuilt.
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
#### Useful Commands

- `/chronicle:standup` creates a standup report from recent sessions.
- `/chronicle:tips` gives workflow advice based on your own usage patterns.
- `/chronicle:cost-tips` highlights ways to reduce token usage and cost.
- `/chronicle:search <query>` finds sessions by keyword, file path, PR, or issue.
- Free-form questions like `What files did I edit yesterday?` can also search session history.
:::
::::

---

## Chat Participants

::: {.slash-command-table}
| Participant | Best use | Example prompt |
|---|---|---|
| `@github` | PRs, issues, repositories, and GitHub activity | `@github show the recent merged PRs in this repo` |
| `@terminal` | shell commands and terminal output | `@terminal explain why this command failed` |
| `@vscode` | VS Code features, settings, and extension APIs | `@vscode how do I configure a launch.json for Python?` |
| Extension participants | domain-specific helpers added by extensions | use these when a tool or platform adds its own specialist participant |
:::

Participants steer the conversation toward a domain. Tools give the chat session capabilities.

# Part 9: GitHub Copilot CLI

## Github Copilot Cli installation

GitHub Copilot CLI is Copilot's terminal interface. 
It reached **General Availability on February 25, 2026**.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Install & Launch
```bash
# macOS / Linux
npm install -g @github/copilot

# launch from the folder you want to work in
copilot
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### First Things To Know

- Launch `copilot` in a folder that contains the code you want to work with
- By default, Copilot CLI uses **Claude Sonnet 4.5**
- Use `/login` inside the session for OAuth but `GH_TOKEN` also works for automation
:::
::::

---

## Bring Your Own Model: Copilot CLI + Ollama

Use Ollama when you want Copilot CLI to run against your own local or hosted model instead of the default provider.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Quick Start

```bash
ollama launch copilot
ollama launch copilot --model qwen3.5

# headless / CI-style
ollama launch copilot --model kimi-k2.5:cloud --yes -- -p "how does this repository work?"
```

- `ollama launch copilot` is the shortest path to a working session
- pass `--model` when you want a specific local or cloud model
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Manual Wiring

```bash
export COPILOT_PROVIDER_BASE_URL=http://localhost:11434/v1
export COPILOT_PROVIDER_API_KEY=
export COPILOT_PROVIDER_WIRE_API=responses
export COPILOT_MODEL=qwen3.5
copilot
```

- Copilot CLI talks to Ollama through its OpenAI-compatible API
- prefer models with a large context window; Ollama recommends at least 64k tokens for Copilot CLI
- examples from the docs: `qwen3.5`, `glm-4.7-flash`, `glm-5:cloud`, `kimi-k2.5:cloud`
:::
::::

Source: <https://docs.ollama.com/integrations/copilot-cli>

---

## CLI Workflows And Built-In Agents

### Choose The Workflow

::: {.slash-command-table}
| Workflow | Trigger | Model | Choose it when |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coding | normal interactive session | `claude-sonnet-4.5` | implement changes, run tools, and iterate |
| Rubber Duck | automatic checkpoints or ask Copilot to critique its work | any Claude model with GPT-5.4 access enabled | get a second opinion after planning, complex implementations, or tests |
| Review | `/review` | `claude-sonnet-4.5` | inspect a diff for risks, regressions, or missing tests |
| Research | `/research TOPIC` | `claude-sonnet-4.6` | run a slower repo, GitHub, and web investigation with a saved report |
| Delegation | `/tasks`, `/fleet` | `claude-haiku-4.5` | hand off long-running or parallel work |

:::

- To start using Rubber Duck, install GitHub Copilot CLI and run `/experimental`.
- It becomes available when you select any Claude model from the model picker and have access enabled to GPT-5.4.
- Critiques surface automatically at checkpoints or on demand when you ask Copilot to critique its work; Copilot incorporates the feedback and shows what changed.

---

## Main Session Commands

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Everyday Control

- `/model` to view or switch models mid-session
- `/agent <name>` to load a custom agent
- `/add-dir <path>` to expand working context
- `/remote [on|off]` exposes the live session on GitHub.com or GitHub Mobile so you can keep steering it from another device
- `/session` or `/usage` shows usage metrics for the current CLI session. Use them to audit activity, review model or tool usage, and debug a run
- `/diff` and `/review` to inspect changes
- `!<command>` to run a shell command inline
- `/mcp show|add|auth` manages MCP servers
- `/undo` or `/rewind` reverts the last turn and file changes
- `/terminal-setup` fixes multiline input support in terminals that need it

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Agentic And Ops

- `/tasks` checks background work
- `/allow-all` or `/yolo` removes approval prompts, so only use it in trusted environments
:::
::::

Slash commands cheat sheet: <https://github.blog/ai-and-ml/github-copilot/a-cheat-sheet-to-slash-commands-in-github-copilot-cli/>

---

## Manage Context Deliberately

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Manual Control With `/compact`

- Use `/compact` any time you want Copilot to compress the current context
- This helps keep long-running sessions focused instead of carrying every earlier turn forward
- Press `Escape` if you start the command and want to cancel it
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Visualize Usage With `/context`

- `/context` shows a detailed token usage breakdown for the current session
- Use it to see how your context window is being consumed
- It is the fastest way to understand whether files, history, or instructions are taking most of the budget
:::
::::

---

## Recovery And Session Controls In The Chat UI

Use these once a Copilot chat session is already open.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Resume The Right Session

- `/resume [VALUE]` lets you switch sessions from a list or jump directly by session ID, ID prefix, or session name
- `/continue` jumps back into the most recent session
- Use these when you want to pick up work without starting a fresh context window
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Inspect And Manage Sessions

- Bare `/session` matches `/usage` for current-session metrics; `/session ...` and `/sessions ...` manage saved sessions
- Key subcommands include `info`, `checkpoints [n]`, `files`, `plan`, `rename [NAME]`, `cleanup`, `prune`, `delete [ID]`, and `delete-all`
- `/session checkpoints` lists session checkpoints; `/session checkpoints NUMBER` shows the checkpoint you want to display
- `/session files` shows temporary files created during the current session, including artifacts that should not be saved to the repository
- `/session plan` shows the current plan, if Copilot has generated one
:::
::::

---

## Share Or Export Session Output

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Available Forms

```bash
/share [file|html|gist] [session|research] [PATH]
/export [file|html|gist] [session|research] [PATH]
```

- Share a live session or a research report as Markdown, interactive HTML, or a GitHub gist
- `session` targets the current conversation, while `research` targets a saved research report
- Use `PATH` when you want a specific output file location
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Practical Uses

- `/share html session` creates a browsable interactive export of the current session
- `/share gist research` is useful when you want to send research findings quickly
- `/export file session notes.md` saves a Markdown artifact for docs, tickets, or handoff notes
- Treat `/share` and `/export` as the publishing layer for work done in the CLI
:::
::::

---

## Experimental Mode Controls

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Enabling Experimental Mode
```bash
copilot --experimental    # launch with experimental mode
# or inside a session:
/experimental             # enable it from the CLI
```

Experimental mode enables features that are still in development, including fleet orchestration, history tools, and power-user session controls.
Once you enable it, the setting is persisted in your config, so later launches no longer need the `--experimental` flag.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Why Enable It

- Use it when you need commands or controls marked as experimental
- Turn it on early in workshops or demos so advanced commands are already available
- Treat it as an opt-in feature gate for newer CLI capabilities

This is the switch that exposes the more advanced side of the CLI.
:::
::::

---

## Steer A Session From Another Device With `/remote`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Command Forms

```bash
/remote
/remote on
/remote off
copilot --remote
```

- `/remote on` enables remote steering for the current interactive session
- plain `/remote` shows status and re-displays the access details for the running session
- `/remote off` disables remote access when you are done
- `copilot --remote` starts a session with remote steering enabled from launch
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### When To Use It

- Use it when the original CLI session is still running but you need to monitor output, answer prompts, or approve actions from another device
- The original machine must stay online, and the session must stay open in the terminal
- Remote steering only works inside a GitHub-hosted repository and is limited to the same GitHub account that started the session
- Pair it with `/keep-alive` when you plan to step away from the host machine

This turns an interactive CLI session into something you can keep driving from GitHub.com or GitHub Mobile.
:::
::::

## Keep The Machine Awake With `/keep-alive`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Command Forms
```bash
/keep-alive on
/keep-alive busy
/keep-alive 30m
/keep-alive 2h
```

- `on` prevents sleep while the CLI session stays active
- `busy` prevents sleep only while the agent is actively working
- `NUMBERm` or `NUMBERh` keeps the machine awake for a fixed time window
- This command is only available in experimental mode
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Why It Matters

- Use it for long reviews, research runs, and unattended automation
- `busy` is the safest default when you only want to protect active work
- Time-based windows help when you are stepping away from the keyboard
- Session-wide `on` is useful for workshops, demos, or extended pairing

This is where the CLI starts to feel like a durable runtime instead of a one-shot prompt tool.
:::
::::

---

## Run Tasks In Parallel With `/fleet`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
### Using The Slash Command

```bash
/fleet [PROMPT]
```

- Enter `/fleet` followed by the prompt you want Copilot to execute
- Use it after you already have a plan and want autonomous implementation
- `/fleet` lets Copilot use subagents and parallelize independent work where possible
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
### When To Reach For It

- Best fit: implementation plans that can be broken into review, coding, and validation tracks
- It is stronger than a normal prompt when the work can branch and rejoin
- Expect Copilot to keep working without constant input, but still ask when a decision is needed

Think of `/fleet` as the handoff from planning to execution.
:::
::::

---

## Typical `/fleet` Workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
### From Plan Mode To Execution

1. In an interactive CLI session, press `Shift+Tab` to switch into plan mode.
2. Describe the feature or change you want to make.
3. Work with Copilot to produce an implementation plan.
4. When the plan looks right, choose how to launch execution.
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
### Two Common Handoffs

- Accept plan and build on autopilot, then use `/fleet`
- Exit plan mode, then run a prompt such as `/fleet implement the plan`

In both cases, Copilot starts working through the plan and can delegate parts of the work to subagents in parallel.
:::
::::

---

## Monitor Fleet Work With `/tasks`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### What You See

- Run `/tasks` to list background tasks for the current session
- The list includes subagent work created by `/fleet`
- Completed subtasks show a summary of what was done when you open them
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Keyboard Controls

- `Up` and `Down`: move through the task list
- `Enter`: open task details
- `k`: kill a running task
- `r`: remove a completed or killed task from the list

`/tasks` is the operational view for long-running or parallel work.
:::
::::

---

## Session History With `/chronicle`

  `/chronicle` is easy to miss because it sits behind **experimental mode**, but it is one of the most useful CLI features.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### What It Uses

- Every CLI session is saved locally
- Session files live under `~/.copilot/session-state/`
- A local SQLite store powers history queries and summaries
- Nothing extra is uploaded beyond normal model interactions
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Main Subcommands

```bash
/chronicle standup
/chronicle tips
/chronicle improve
/chronicle reindex
```

- `standup` summarizes recent work
- `tips` gives workflow advice
- `improve` suggests better custom instructions
- `reindex` rebuilds the local session store
:::
::::

### When `/chronicle` Helps

- Start of day: `/chronicle standup last 3 days`
- Repeated misunderstandings: `/chronicle improve`
- Workflow coaching: `/chronicle tips`
- Restoring local history after cleanup: `/chronicle reindex`

The key idea is simple: Copilot CLI can reason over your own local session history.

## Reference Shortcuts Worth Teaching

- `@ filename` attaches file contents to context
- `# 123` attaches a GitHub issue or PR
- `?` opens quick help on an empty prompt
- `Ctrl+G` opens the prompt in `$EDITOR`
- `Ctrl+X` then `/` runs a slash command without losing a drafted prompt
- `Ctrl+Enter` or `Ctrl+Q` queues a message while the agent is busy
- `Shift+Tab` cycles execution modes
- `Ctrl+T` toggles reasoning display in the timeline

---

## Steer The Conversation While Copilot Is Thinking

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Queue Follow-Up Messages

- Send another message while Copilot is still working to steer it in a new direction
- `Ctrl+Enter` or `Ctrl+Q` queues the message until the current response finishes
- This keeps the session natural: you do not have to wait for a full answer before correcting course
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Give Feedback On Rejected Tool Requests

- If you reject a permission request, add inline feedback explaining why
- Copilot can adapt its plan instead of stopping outright
- This is useful when you want a safer command, narrower scope, or a different approach
:::
::::

---

## Non-Interactive Mode For Scripts And CI

Use these commands while you are still in the shell, before an interactive session starts or a scripted run begins.

::: {.slash-command-table}
| Command | What it is for |
|---|---|
| `copilot` | Launch the interactive user interface in the current folder. |
| `copilot help [TOPIC]` | Show CLI help. Useful topics include `config`, `commands`, `environment`, `logging`, `monitoring`, `permissions`, and `providers`. |
| `copilot init` | Initialize Copilot custom instructions for the current repository. |
| `copilot login [OPTION]` | Authenticate with Copilot via the OAuth device flow. Use `--host HOST` when you need to sign in against GitHub Enterprise instead of `https://github.com`. |
| `copilot mcp` | Manage MCP server configurations from the command line instead of from inside chat. |
| `copilot plugin` | Manage plugins and plugin marketplaces. |
| `copilot update` | Download and install the latest CLI version. |
| `copilot version` | Display version information and check whether an update is available. |
:::

Rule of thumb: use these commands to start, configure, authenticate, extend, or update Copilot; use the next slide when the shell invocation itself should answer and exit.

---

## Programmatic Github Copilot Cli Usage

Copilot CLI also has a **non-interactive prompt mode** for pipelines, automation, and programmatic use.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Useful Flags
```bash
copilot -p "task"             # non-interactive prompt mode
-s, --silent                  # output only the agent response
--output-format json          # emit machine-readable JSON
--additional-mcp-config @f.json  # inject MCP servers per run
--list-env                    # debug loaded agents/skills/plugins
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### CI Shape
```yaml
copilot-review:
  stage: review
  script:
    - copilot -p "Review $CI_COMMIT_SHA for regressions"
        --silent --output-format json
        | jq -r '.message' >> review.md
  artifacts:
    paths: [review.md]
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"
```

- Store `GH_TOKEN` as a masked CI/CD variable
- Consume JSON output with `jq`
- Use this mode for review, triage, and reporting jobs
:::
::::

---

## Command line options

Use these when you launch Copilot from the shell or script a run.

- `--agent=AGENT` starts directly in a chosen custom agent
- `--additional-mcp-config=@file.json` injects MCP servers for one run
- `--allow-all`, `--allow-all-tools`, `--allow-tool=...` control permission prompts
- `--autopilot` enables autonomous continuation in prompt mode
- `--available-tools=...` constrains what the model can use
- `--resume` reopens the session selector from the command line
- `--continue` jumps back into the most recent session
- `-p` or `--prompt` passes a single prompt on the command line. Copilot completes the task and then exits
- `-s` or `--silent` outputs only the agent response, without usage statistics, which makes it easier to script around `-p`. Add an approval flag when the run needs to edit files or execute tools

## Basic Example of usages

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Direct Prompt Mode
```bash
copilot -p "Show me this week's commits and summarize them" \
  --allow-tool='shell(git)' \
  --silent
```

:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Pipes And Existing Sessions
```bash
./script-outputting-options.sh | copilot

```

:::
::::


:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Scope Shell Access Tightly
```bash
# allow all git commands except git push
copilot -p "Summarize the current branch history" \
  --allow-tool='shell(git:*)' \
  --deny-tool='shell(git push)'
```

:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Allow MCP Tools Precisely
```bash
# allow a specific MCP tool
copilot -p "Open a backlog issue" \
  --allow-tool='MyMCP(create_issue)'

# allow every tool from one server
copilot -p "Work with the ticketing system" \
  --allow-tool='MyMCP'
```

:::
::::

---

## Use Custom Agents In Prompt Mode

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Delegate To A Named Agent
```bash
copilot -p "Review the latest commit" \
  --allow-tool='shell' \
  --agent code-review
```

- `--agent` sends the prompt to a named custom agent
- The agent must already exist in your Copilot CLI agent files
- This is the programmatic way to steer work toward a specialized persona
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Important Distinction

- `--agent code-review` only works if you created a custom agent with that exact name
- That is different from the built-in review workflow exposed through `/review`
- Use custom agents when you want persistent instructions, restricted tools, or a fixed role across many runs
:::
::::

---

## Choose Models For Prompt Runs

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Pick A Model Per Run
```bash
copilot -p "What does this project do?" \
  -s --model claude-haiku-4.5

copilot -p "Fix the race condition in the worker pool" \
  --model gpt-5.3-codex \
  --allow-tool='write, shell'
```

- `--model` lets you trade off speed, cost, and capability for each run
- Without `-s` or `--silent`, the CLI shows which model produced the response
- `copilot help` lists the exact model strings you can use
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Persist A Default Model
```bash
export COPILOT_MODEL=claude-haiku-4.5
```

```json
{
  "model": "gpt-5.3-codex",
  "effortLevel": "low"
}
```

- `COPILOT_MODEL` sets the default model for the current shell session
- Persist defaults in `~/.copilot/settings.json` or `$COPILOT_HOME/settings.json`
- Some models support `effortLevel` to control how much reasoning time they spend
:::
::::

---

## Useful Programmatic Recipes

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Review And Generate
```bash
copilot -p '/review the changes on this branch compared to main. Focus on bugs and security issues.' \
  -s --allow-tool='shell(git:*)'

copilot -p 'Generate JSDoc comments for all exported functions in src/api/' \
  --allow-tool=write
```

- The first example runs the built-in review workflow against the current branch
- The second uses prompt mode for batch documentation work
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Export The Result
```bash
copilot -p "Audit this project's dependencies for vulnerabilities" \
  --allow-tool='shell(npm:*), shell(npx:*)' \
  --share='./audit-report.md'

copilot -p 'Summarize the architecture of this project' --share-gist
```

- `--share=PATH` saves the full session transcript to a local Markdown file
- `--share-gist` publishes the transcript as a GitHub gist for quick sharing
- Gists are not available for Enterprise Managed Users or GitHub Enterprise Cloud data residency environments
:::
::::

---

## Shell Scripting Patterns

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
### Capture Or Branch On Output
```bash
result=$(copilot -p 'What version of Node.js does this project require?
Give the number only. No other text.' -s)
echo "Required Node version: $result"
```

```bash
if copilot -p 'Does this project have any TypeScript errors? Reply only YES or NO.' -s \
  | grep -qi "no"; then
  echo "No type errors found."
else
  echo "Type errors detected."
fi
```
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Process Many Files
```bash
for file in src/api/*.ts; do
  echo "--- Reviewing $file ---" | tee -a review-results.md
  copilot -p "Review $file for error handling issues" -s --allow-all-tools | tee -a review-results.md
done
```

- `-s` keeps the output easy to capture, parse, and append in shell pipelines
- Loops and conditionals work well when prompts are narrow and output is constrained
- For unattended runs, narrow tool permissions are safer than `--allow-all-tools` when possible
:::
::::

## MCP In Programmatic CLI Runs

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
### Built-In MCP Servers

| Server | What it gives you |
|---|---|
| `github-mcp-server` | GitHub API access for issues, pull requests, commits, code search, and GitHub Actions |
| `playwright` | Browser automation for navigation, clicks, typing, screenshots, and forms |
| `fetch` | HTTP requests through the `fetch` tool |
| `time` | Time utilities such as `get_current_time` and `convert_time` |

These are available in the CLI without extra setup.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
### Trust Levels

| Source | Trust | Review |
|---|---|---|
| Built-in | High | No |
| Repository (`.github/mcp.json`) | Medium | Recommended |
| Workspace (`.mcp.json`) | Medium | Recommended |
| User config (`~/.copilot/mcp-config.json`) | User-defined | Your responsibility |
| Remote servers | Low | Always |
:::
::::

If your project still uses VS Code's `.vscode/mcp.json`, migrate it to the CLI's `.mcp.json` format by remapping `servers` to `mcpServers`:

```bash
jq '{mcpServers: .servers}' .vscode/mcp.json > .mcp.json
```

---

## Getting Help

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="46%"}
### Quick CLI Help

From the command line, display help with:

```bash
copilot -h
```

- Use this when you want a fast reminder of the main CLI commands and flags
:::
::: {.column width="54%"}
### Help By Topic

For help on specific topics, enter:

```bash
copilot help TOPIC
```

- Common topics include `config`, `commands`, `environment`, `logging`, and `permissions`
- Use topic help when you need more detail on setup, runtime behavior, or CLI permissions
:::
::::

# Questions?

# Part 10: AI-Assisted Coding Training

{{< include github-copilot-training/slides.body.qmd >}}
